example_english: They say love is Tamil itself.
era_desc: One of the oldest recorded Tamil emotional concepts, found throughout Sangam poetry (Akananuru, Purananuru).

example_english: The sky is blue in colour.
era_desc: Common in classical Sangam verse as a symbol of boundlessness and the divine canopy over the earth.

example_english: Flowers have bloomed in the garden.
era_desc: Appears across all five tinai (landscape) categories in Sangam literature, especially Mullai and Kurinji.

example_english: The sound of the sea can be heard.
era_desc: Central to the Neytal tinai. Appears in Sangam texts like Natrinai and Akananuru as a symbol of longing.

example_english: It is raining well.
era_desc: Key seasonal symbol in the Mullai tinai. Associated with reunion of lovers in Sangam poetry.

example_english: He/She sang a sweet song.
era_desc: The oral tradition of Tamil song predates written records. Paadam (song) is foundational to Sangam culture.

example_english: The moon is very bright today.
era_desc: The moon as witness to love is a recurring image in Kurinji and Neytal poetry of the Sangam corpus.

example_english: A child sleeping in the mother's lap.
era_desc: Among the oldest Tamil root words. Found in Tolkappiyam as a fundamental kinship term.

example_english: Every word has a meaning.
era_desc: Discussed by Tolkappiyam (c. 300 BCE) as a foundational linguistic unit of Tamil grammar.

example_english: I read a book every day.
era_desc: Sanskrit loanword that entered Tamil during the Pallava and early Chola periods of Sanskrit-Tamil literary exchange.

example_english: Education is the foundation of human life.
era_desc: Sanskrit-origin term that spread widely through Bhakti literature and temple inscriptions of the Pallava era.

example_english: She has great happiness.
era_desc: Popularised through the Bhakti movement hymns of Thevaram and Divya Prabandham (7th–9th century CE).

example_english: Love is the beauty of life.
era_desc: The concept of romantic love (akam) is central to Sangam poetry. Kaadhal appears in Ainkurunooru and Natrinai.

example_english: My friend came with me.
era_desc: Friendship and camaraderie are themes in the Puram (public) poetry of the Sangam anthologies.

example_english: He/She writes beautiful poetry.
era_desc: Though poetry existed since Sangam times, the Sanskrit-derived word 'kavithai' became common during the medieval Tamil-Sanskrit bilingual tradition.

meaning_english: River; also: to cool or soothe
example_english: The Kaveri river is the lifeline of Tamil Nadu.
era_desc: Rivers were sacred and foundational in early Tamil geography. The word appears in Tolkappiyam and early Sangam texts.

example_english: That tree gives shade.
era_desc: One of the earliest nature words in Tamil. Trees played a central role in tinai classification in Tolkappiyam.

example_english: Educational knowledge is the greatest wealth.
era_desc: Education and learning are praised throughout Sangam poetry and in Thirukkural (c. 100 BCE – 300 CE).

example_english: The long journey was tiring.
era_desc: Travel and separation form the core of Akam poetry. Journey imagery is central to the Palai tinai.

example_english: This world is very beautiful.
era_desc: Purananuru opens with the concept of the world as home to all people. The word appears prominently in Sangam political poetry.

example_english: Building a good society is necessary.
era_desc: Arabic-origin word that entered Tamil through trade and later standardised in modern Tamil during colonial-era social reform writings.

example_english: The child is speaking in baby talk.
era_desc: Children and childhood are tenderly depicted in Sangam poetry, especially in Purananuru and Akananuru.

example_english: The mind finds peace when listening to music.
era_desc: Music (isai) was one of the three pillars of Sangam civilisation alongside literature (ilakkiyam) and dance (natam).

example_english: There are many Sangam literary texts.
era_desc: The word 'nool' meaning literary text is used by Tolkappiyar himself to describe his grammar. It signifies the ancient academic tradition.

example_english: Victory can be achieved through hard work.
era_desc: Victory in battle is a central theme of Puram poetry. Celebrated in Purananuru and the Pattupattu anthologies.

example_english: Faith propels a person forward.
era_desc: Developed its modern emotional sense through Bhakti devotional literature, especially the Nayanmars and Alwars.

example_english: The beauty of nature cannot be described in words.
era_desc: Beauty (azagu) is a profound Sangam concept — not merely aesthetic but moral and spiritual, discussed in Thirukkural.

example_english: The light of the sun helps all living beings.
era_desc: Light as metaphor for knowledge and the divine appears throughout Sangam texts and early Shaiva Siddhanta writings.

example_english: His voice was very melodious.
era_desc: The human voice as instrument of poetry and music is celebrated in the Sangam anthologies Kalithokai and Akananuru.

example_english: Education is the greatest wealth.
era_desc: Wealth and its proper use is a key topic of Thirukkural. The tension between material and spiritual wealth runs through Sangam literature.

example_english: Tamil is one of the oldest languages.
era_desc: The word 'mozhi' is fundamental to Tamil linguistic identity. Tolkappiyam analyses mozhi as the basis of all human communication.

example_english: Truth alone conquers the world.
era_desc: Truth (unmai) is the highest value in Thirukkural's first chapter on Aram (virtue). It recurs across Sangam ethical poetry.

example_english: Many kinds of animals live in the forest.
era_desc: The forest (Palai and Mullai tinai) is one of the five ancient Tamil landscapes. A foundational ecological and poetic concept.

example_english: If you sow a seed in the soil, a tree will sprout.
era_desc: Earth and soil are among the oldest Tamil root concepts. The Tamil land (Mann) is a deeply emotional and political symbol.

example_english: Fire uncontrolled is dangerous.
era_desc: Fire rituals and the sacred fire appear in early Sangam and Vedic-influenced Tamil texts. One of the five classical elements.

example_english: There is no life without water.
era_desc: Water (neer) is one of the five classical elements. The Neytal landscape is defined by the sea. Appears in Tolkappiyam.

example_english: A cool breeze is blowing.
era_desc: Wind (kaatru) is one of the five classical elements. The Marutham tinai is associated with cool river-bank breezes.

example_english: Earth is our home.
era_desc: Sanskrit-origin word for earth that entered Tamil through Pallava-era inscriptions and Shaiva temple literature.

example: அவளுக்கு படிக்க வேண்டும் என்ற ஆசை உண்டு.
example_english: She has a desire to study.
era_desc: Desire and longing are the emotional core of Akam (love) poetry. Aasai appears frequently in Natrinai and Ainkurunooru.

example_english: I had a sweet dream.
era_desc: Dreams are poetic devices in Sangam Akam poetry, where the beloved appears in the sleeping lover's vision.

example_english: If the mind is clear, everything is fine.
era_desc: The mind (manam) is theorised in Tolkappiyam's Porulathikaram as the seat of all emotional experience in Akam poetry.

example_english: Bravery is an adornment for a person.
era_desc: Heroism (veeram) is celebrated in Puram poetry. The seven vekai (heroic categories) are detailed in Purananuru and Tolkappiyam.

example_english: No one understood his grief.
era_desc: Grief and suffering are the emotional core of Sangam separation poetry, especially in the Palai tinai of Akananuru.

example_english: Words that pierce the heart cannot be forgotten.
era_desc: The chest as the seat of the heart and emotion is a recurring motif in Sangam Akam poetry and Thirukkural.

example_english: Grandfather is telling a story.
era_desc: The oral storytelling tradition evolved into written narrative during the medieval period, seen in Kambaramayanam and Periyapuranam.

example_english: Laughter is good for the body.
era_desc: Humour and laughter (hasya) emerged as a recognised poetic rasa in medieval Tamil literature, especially in Prabandham genres.

example_english: Life itself is a poem.
era_desc: Life and its meaning are the subject of Thirukkural's entire first section (Aram). The word appears from the earliest Tamil texts.

example_english: Even nectar tastes bitter to the hungry.
era_desc: Hunger and poverty are unflinchingly depicted in Sangam Puram poetry, where the poet-patron relationship addresses material need.

example_english: Learning a handicraft is good.
era_desc: Occupations and trade are documented in Sangam poetry. The five tinai landscapes each had associated occupations and livelihoods.

example_english: Tamil history is ancient.
era_desc: Historical writing (varalaru) developed formally in the Chola period with texts like Kulottunga Cholan Pillai Thamizh and copper-plate inscriptions.
